meta.title: Mood Decline after Temporal Lobe Surgery
meta.outcome: Predicted probability of clinically significant postoperative mood decline (a ≥15% rise in Beck Depression Inventory-II with a postoperative raw score >11) in adults undergoing temporal lobe resection for pharmacoresistant epilepsy, from six preoperative predictors.
meta.cff_info:
What this shows. This calculator reproduces the nomogram of Doherty et al. (Epilepsia 2021) estimating the probability of clinically significant mood decline after temporal lobe resection (TLR) for drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy. Mood decline is defined as a ≥15% increase in the Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II) with a postoperative raw score >11. It uses six preoperative variables: psychiatric history, side (dominance) of resection, verbal fluency (F-A-S) score, relationship status, age, and presence of a malformation of cortical development (MCD) on MRI.

How to read it

  • The result is the percent chance of clinically significant mood decline (0–100) after surgery.
  • Preoperative psychiatric history is the strongest driver — it roughly triples the odds of decline.
  • Being divorced (and not in a new relationship), undergoing a dominant-sided resection, and a higher preoperative verbal fluency score increase risk; younger age increases risk; an MRI malformation of cortical development nudges risk upward.

Key findings

  • Developed on 592 patients (492 from the Cleveland Clinic plus 100 from four other North American centers). 14% of the Cleveland cohort and 22% of the external cohort had clinically significant mood decline.
  • Discrimination was moderate — optimism-adjusted c-statistic 0.70 — with good calibration for predicted risks up to 40%; above 40% the model slightly overestimates risk.
  • Psychiatric history (OR 2.75), divorced status (OR 2.97 vs single), dominant resection (OR 1.92), and each additional verbal-fluency point (OR 1.04) were the notable predictors; age and MCD were retained to improve model performance.
  • Postoperative seizure recurrence and cognitive (auditory immediate memory) decline were also associated with mood worsening but are outcomes, not preoperative inputs, so are not part of this tool.

Demonstration and counseling aid — not a substitute for a multidisciplinary epilepsy surgery and psychiatric evaluation.

predictors.name: Psychiatric history
predictors.assess: Any preoperative psychiatric diagnosis (e.g. depression, anxiety, substance use), coded from the preoperative psychology/psychiatry notes or medical history. This is the strongest predictor of postoperative mood decline (β = 1.010; OR 2.75, 95% CI 1.60–4.70).

predictors.name: Side of resection
predictors.assess: Language-dominant vs nondominant temporal lobe resection, determined by side of surgery plus language lateralization (fMRI/Wada). Dominant-sided resection roughly doubles the odds of mood decline (β = 0.652; OR 1.92, 95% CI 1.19–3.09).

predictors.name: Verbal fluency score
predictors.assess: Preoperative phonemic (letter) verbal fluency — total words generated for the letters F, A and S (F-A-S raw score), a measure of executive function. Counter-intuitively, higher preoperative fluency predicts greater risk of mood decline: each additional point raises the odds ~4% (β = 0.036; OR 1.04, 95% CI 1.02–1.06), i.e. ~40% higher odds per 10-point increase.

predictors.name: Relationship status
predictors.assess: Current relationship status (reference = single). Patients coded as divorced or widowed but in a new relationship are counted as 'in a relationship'. Divorced patients not in a new relationship are at highest risk (β = 1.090; OR 2.97 vs single, 95% CI 1.27–6.96); being in a relationship or widowed is protective.

predictors.name: Age
predictors.assess: Age (years) at the time of surgery. In this cohort younger age increased the risk of mood decline (β = −0.021; OR 0.98 per year), likely because psychiatric history and relationship status act as confounders. Development-cohort median 39 years.

predictors.name: Malformation of cortical development on MRI
predictors.assess: Presence of a malformation of cortical development (MCD) on MRI — e.g. focal cortical dysplasia, gray-matter heterotopia, polymicrogyria, schizencephaly. Its presence increases estimated risk (β = 0.569; OR 1.77, 95% CI 0.83–3.75, not independently significant but retained to improve model performance).

model.panel_title: Chance of clinically significant mood decline

outputs.label: Chance of clinically significant mood decline

model.note: Percent chance of a clinically significant mood decline (≥15% rise in BDI-II with postoperative raw score >11) after temporal lobe resection. Preoperative psychiatric history is the strongest driver. Doherty et al., Epilepsia 2021 (optimism-adjusted c-statistic 0.70).
model.viz_info: Each predictor contributes its published logistic weight (β × value for the age/verbal-fluency sliders; the categorical options add their β). The weights plus the model intercept (−3.318) are summed and passed through a logistic function to give the percent chance of mood decline. The waterfall shows how each factor pushes the estimate up or down; the donut shows the resulting probability. Note: the paper reports the model slightly overestimates risk above ~40%.

provenance.source: Doherty 2021 Epilepsia Table 2 (published logistic coefficients + intercept) and Figure 1 nomogram — PMID 33368200, doi 10.1111/epi.16800.
provenance.notes: Logistic model (Firth-penalized): LP = -3.318 + 1.010·[psych history] + 0.652·[dominant] + 0.036·verbal_fluency + [relationship: widowed -1.228 / in-relationship -0.543 / single 0 / married 0.326 / divorced 1.090] - 0.021·age + 0.569·[MCD present]; pct = 100/(1+exp(-LP)). Coefficients & intercept from Table 2; relationship reference = single. Plausibility check: psych-history Yes, dominant, fluency 35, single, age 39, MCD absent → LP -1.215 → 22.9% (consistent with external-cohort 22% base rate). Optimism-adjusted c-statistic 0.70; well calibrated to ~40%, slight overestimation above 40%. Third of the Cleveland Clinic surgical-neuropsych nomogram trio (naming/memory/mood). Extracted 2026-07-08.
